message.content: mount BV

addedBlocks:
## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  
  Experiment, now largely proven — continuation requiring judgment. Read the ₣BV
  paddock first, but note its central thesis is partly disproven (below) and the
  paddock itself owes a curry.
  
  ## Landed (see this pace's commits — jjx_log BV)
  
  theurge runs the runtime-free fast tier green on an uncontrolled Cygwin
  (cygwin@rocket), save only the pre-existing violation under Remaining. The
  launch and platform boundaries are solved, correcting the paddock thesis:
  
  - bash does NOT export `$OSTYPE` to native children, so RBTDRX sat inert.
    Platform now arrives as a synthesized BURD regime member, `BURD_OSTYPE`
    (bud_dispatch + burd_regime); theurge's `rbtdrx_is_cygwin` reads it.
  - A bare `bash` from a Windows-native binary resolves to System32's WSL
    launcher, never Cygwin's. theurge now derives the Cygwin bash via cygpath
    (`rbtdri_bash_program`), reusing RBTDRX's existing cygpath dependency.
  - Also: cargo on the ssh PATH via rocket's `~/.bash_profile`; cargo
    `--manifest-path` translation in `rbte_engine.sh`.
  
  ## Remaining (in scope)
  
  - Repair the pre-existing `cat` command-discipline violation in
    `Tools/rbk/rbgo_OAuth.sh` (BCG eviction table → bash builtin). It is what the
    kit-bash fixture (`rbtdru_kit_bash`) flags, and the last thing between here and
    a fully green fast suite. Not introduced by this heat, but fixed under it.
  
  ## Known gap (likely a follow-on, given the heat's locked runtime-free scope)
  
  A whole cloud process has not been exercised from Cygwin — only the
  runtime-free fast tier is proven. Recorded so it is not forgotten; whether it
  belongs here or in a new heat is an operator call.
  
  ## Done
  
  Fast suite green over cygwin@rocket, kit-bash included, after the `cat` repair.

lastPrompt: mount BV

content.text: Now let me read the paddock from the gazette before diving into the docket.

content.content:
1	# jjezs_paddock ₣BV
2	
3	## Shape
4	
5	Prove that Recipe Bottle's test harness (theurge) can run on an **uncontrolled**
6	Cygwin — a stock Windows+Cygwin box we do not provision. This is a consumer-
7	credibility claim: a consumer can qualify their own install on their own machine
8	and trust the verdict. It is deliberately distinct from the `rbk-NN-win-*` heats,
9	which are all controlled-infrastructure work (formal BURN garrison, remote
10	control, depot regime) on machines we own.
11	
12	## Central thesis (the real problem)
13	
14	The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
15	(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
16	Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
17	bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
18	**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:
19	
20	- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
21	  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
22	  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
23	  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
24	- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
25	  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
26	  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
27	  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
28	  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.
29	
30	## Locked decisions
31	
32	- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
33	  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
34	  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
35	  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
36	  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
37	  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
38	- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
39	  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the
40	  "correct" answer — but it ships no precompiled std (needs nightly `-Z
41	  build-std`), and requires Cygwin `gcc` + LLVM ≥20.1. Fall back to it only if
42	  the native-binary path translation proves too leaky to finish Route A.
43	- **Toolchain installs are deferred to the experiment, not the dep pace.** The
44	  dep-install pace carries only route-independent knowns; `gcc`/`rust`/LLVM are
45	  Route-C-specific and must not be installed until/unless Route A is abandoned.
46	- **Scope is the runtime-free tier.** Container tiers (service/crucible) need a
47	  real container runtime; on Cygwin the only `docker` is Windows Docker Desktop,
48	  with a separate bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX does not cover.
49	  Per existing discipline, container tests run on the WSL side. This heat
50	  targets the **fast/validation** tier only.
51	
52	## What done looks like
53	
54	`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` runs green over `cygwin@rocket`. A legitimate
55	alternative outcome is honest: the launch-boundary fix is proven and the
56	remaining work scoped, with heavier surgery deferred — the experiment is
57	exploratory and may reveal more than the release timeline wants to absorb.
58	
59	## Known gaps to close
60	
61	- **PATH visibility.** `cargo` exists on the host but is not on the
62	  non-interactive ssh command channel's PATH (rustup wired the Windows user
63	  PATH; the sshd-spawned shell that runs tabtargets does not see it). Make
64	  `.cargo/bin` resolvable to that shell — most robustly via the Windows *system*
65	  PATH or `~/.ssh/environment`.
66	- **The launch-boundary code change** in `rbtdri`/`rbtdrf` (above).
67	- **shellcheck path-argument boundary (deferred — off the fast-tier critical
68	  path).** `shellcheck` has no native Cygwin package (Haskell/GHC is
69	  incompatible with Cygwin's path layout); the install is the official Windows
70	  `shellcheck.exe` in `/usr/local/bin` (admin-placed — the unprivileged account
71	  cannot write there). It resolves and runs, but as a Windows-native tool it
72	  cannot open Cygwin `/`-style path arguments (`openBinaryFile: does not
73	  exist`) — it needs Windows-form paths (`cygpath -w`) or stdin. This is
74	  RBTDRX-family path translation but a **distinct site** from the `.sh`-launch
75	  boundary above: a Windows *tool* receiving Cygwin path *arguments*, not a
76	  `.sh` being `CreateProcess`'d. It surfaces only when the harness drives
77	  shellcheck in `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease — never in the fast/validation tier this
78	  heat targets. Close it if/when QualifyRelease is brought to Cygwin.
79	
80	# jjezs_pace ₢BVAAB

file.content:
# jjezs_paddock ₣BV

## Shape

Prove that Recipe Bottle's test harness (theurge) can run on an **uncontrolled**
Cygwin — a stock Windows+Cygwin box we do not provision. This is a consumer-
credibility claim: a consumer can qualify their own install on their own machine
and trust the verdict. It is deliberately distinct from the `rbk-NN-win-*` heats,
which are all controlled-infrastructure work (formal BURN garrison, remote
control, depot regime) on machines we own.

## Central thesis (the real problem)

The build system is not the hard part — **the bash-launch boundary is.** RBTDRX
(`rbtdrx_platform.rs`) already solves the *path-string* boundary at theurge's
Rust↔bash crossing, and it is sound: it keys on `OSTYPE=cygwin`, which Cygwin
bash exports to spawned children (verified on `cygwin@rocket`). But RBTDRX does
**not** solve the *process-launch* boundary, and that is where the wall is:

- A Windows-native binary (which `x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` produces) **cannot**
  `CreateProcess` a `.sh` directly — Rust's `std::process::Command` on Windows
  supports only `.exe` (undocumented `.bat`/`.cmd`); a `.sh` yields "not a valid
  Win32 application." This is a documented Rust limitation, not a bug to wait out.
- theurge's invocation layer is inconsistent: some paths route through
  `bash -c` (correct), but the tabtarget-invocation primitives
  (`rbtdri_invoke_impl`, `rbtdrf_run_tt`) exec the `.sh` directly via
  `Command::new(tabtarget)`. Those paths cannot have run green on Cygwin —
  RBTDRX is forward-looking scaffolding and the Cygwin bring-up is unfinished.

## Locked decisions

- **Commit to Route A first — not route-neutral.** Build with the
  already-installed `stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu` toolchain (rustc 1.95.0 at
  `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin`; theurge's deps are pure-Rust, so no C
  toolchain / no system libs needed), and **finish the bring-up by routing every
  tabtarget invocation through `bash` instead of exec'ing the `.sh` directly.**
  RBTDRX stays and handles the path strings.
- **Route C is the documented fallback, not the opening move.** A first-class
  `x86_64-pc-cygwin` target exists (Rust 1.86+, Tier 3) and is semantically the
  "correct" answer — but it ships no precompiled std (needs nightly `-Z
  build-std`), and requires Cygwin `gcc` + LLVM ≥20.1. Fall back to it only if
  the native-binary path translation proves too leaky to finish Route A.
- **Toolchain installs are deferred to the experiment, not the dep pace.** The
  dep-install pace carries only route-independent knowns; `gcc`/`rust`/LLVM are
  Route-C-specific and must not be installed until/unless Route A is abandoned.
- **Scope is the runtime-free tier.** Container tiers (service/crucible) need a
  real container runtime; on Cygwin the only `docker` is Windows Docker Desktop,
  with a separate bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX does not cover.
  Per existing discipline, container tests run on the WSL side. This heat
  targets the **fast/validation** tier only.

## What done looks like

`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` runs green over `cygwin@rocket`. A legitimate
alternative outcome is honest: the launch-boundary fix is proven and the
remaining work scoped, with heavier surgery deferred — the experiment is
exploratory and may reveal more than the release timeline wants to absorb.

## Known gaps to close

- **PATH visibility.** `cargo` exists on the host but is not on the
  non-interactive ssh command channel's PATH (rustup wired the Windows user
  PATH; the sshd-spawned shell that runs tabtargets does not see it). Make
  `.cargo/bin` resolvable to that shell — most robustly via the Windows *system*
  PATH or `~/.ssh/environment`.
- **The launch-boundary code change** in `rbtdri`/`rbtdrf` (above).
- **shellcheck path-argument boundary (deferred — off the fast-tier critical
  path).** `shellcheck` has no native Cygwin package (Haskell/GHC is
  incompatible with Cygwin's path layout); the install is the official Windows
  `shellcheck.exe` in `/usr/local/bin` (admin-placed — the unprivileged account
  cannot write there). It resolves and runs, but as a Windows-native tool it
  cannot open Cygwin `/`-style path arguments (`openBinaryFile: does not
  exist`) — it needs Windows-form paths (`cygpath -w`) or stdin. This is
  RBTDRX-family path translation but a **distinct site** from the `.sh`-launch
  boundary above: a Windows *tool* receiving Cygwin path *arguments*, not a
  `.sh` being `CreateProcess`'d. It surfaces only when the harness drives
  shellcheck in `rbw-tr` QualifyRelease — never in the fast/validation tier this
  heat targets. Close it if/when QualifyRelease is brought to Cygwin.

# jjezs_pace ₢BVAAB

## Character

Experiment, now largely proven — continuation requiring judgment. Read the ₣BV
paddock first, but note its central thesis is partly disproven (below) and the
paddock itself owes a curry.

## Landed (see this pace's commits — jjx_log BV)

theurge runs the runtime-free fast tier green on an uncontrolled Cygwin
(cygwin@rocket), save only the pre-existing violation under Remaining. The
launch and platform boundaries are solved, correcting the paddock thesis:

- bash does NOT export `$OSTYPE` to native children, so RBTDRX sat inert.
  Platform now arrives as a synthesized BURD regime member, `BURD_OSTYPE`
  (bud_dispatch + burd_regime); theurge's `rbtdrx_is_cygwin` reads it.
- A bare `bash` from a Windows-native binary resolves to System32's WSL
  launcher, never Cygwin's. theurge now derives the Cygwin bash via cygpath
  (`rbtdri_bash_program`), reusing RBTDRX's existing cygpath dependency.
- Also: cargo on the ssh PATH via rocket's `~/.bash_profile`; cargo
  `--manifest-path` translation in `rbte_engine.sh`.

## Remaining (in scope)

- Repair the pre-existing `cat` command-discipline violation in
  `Tools/rbk/rbgo_OAuth.sh` (BCG eviction table → bash builtin). It is what the
  kit-bash fixture (`rbtdru_kit_bash`) flags, and the last thing between here and
  a fully green fast suite. Not introduced by this heat, but fixed under it.

## Known gap (likely a follow-on, given the heat's locked runtime-free scope)

A whole cloud process has not been exercised from Cygwin — only the
runtime-free fast tier is proven. Recorded so it is not forgotten; whether it
belongs here or in a new heat is an operator call.

## Done

Fast suite green over cygwin@rocket, kit-bash included, after the `cat` repair.

file.content:
local z_jwt=""
  local z_token=""
  local z_status=0
  local z_err=""
  local z_err_desc=""

  while :; do
    z_attempt=$((z_attempt + 1))

    z_status=0
    z_jwt=$(zrbgo_build_jwt_capture "${z_rbra_file}") || z_status=$?
    test "${z_status}" -eq 0 || return 1

    z_status=0
    z_token=$(zrbgo_exchange_jwt_capture "${z_jwt}") || z_status=$?

    if test "${z_status}" -eq 0 && test -n "${z_token}"; then
      printf '%s\n' "${z_token}"
      return 0
    fi

    # TEMP-FORENSIC ₢BBABL: capture every token-endpoint mint failure to a
    # stable, append-only absolute log so a later successful mint cannot clobber
    # the failing body (the memo's Host-B capture gap, memo-20260527). Purpose:
    # confirm the ACCOUNT_STATE_INVALID token-endpoint shape before writing the
    # touchpoint-2 match. REMOVE once the shape is captured and the match lands.
    { printf '=== rbgo mint fail rbra=%s status=%s ===\n' "${z_rbra_file}" "${z_status}"
      cat "${ZRBGO_CURL_STDERR_FILE}" "${ZRBGO_OAUTH_RESPONSE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null
      printf '\n=== end ===\n'
    } >> /tmp/rbgo_BBABL_forensic.log 2>/dev/null || true

    # Discriminate failure: only the SA-propagation race shapes retry —
    #   `invalid_grant` + `Invalid JWT Signature.`         (fresh-key propagation lag)
    #   `invalid_grant` + `Invalid grant: account not found` (fresh-SA propagation lag)
    # Any other shape fails fast.
    z_err=$(jq -r '.error // ""'             "${ZRBGO_OAUTH_RESPONSE_FILE}" 2>"${ZRBGO_JQ_STDERR_FILE}") || z_err=""
    z_err_desc=$(jq -r '.error_description // ""' "${ZRBGO_OAUTH_RESPONSE_FILE}" 2>"${ZRBGO_JQ_STDERR_FILE}") || z_err_desc=""

    test "${z_err}" = "invalid_grant" || return 1
    case "${z_err_desc}" in
      "Invalid JWT Signature."|"Invalid grant: account not found") ;;
      *) return 1 ;;
    esac

    cp "${ZRBGO_OAUTH_RESPONSE_FILE}" "${ZRBGO_CONSUMER_RETRY_BODY_FILE}" || return 1

    if test "${z_elapsed}" -ge "${RBGC_SA_KEY_CONSUMER_RETRY_BUDGET_SEC}"; then
      buc_warn "Token mint: SA propagation race timeout after ${z_elapsed}s (${z_attempt} attempts); last body: ${ZRBGO_CONSUMER_RETRY_BODY_FILE}"
      return 1
    fi

11:This pattern emerged from environments where hidden failures cascade into production outages. When bash scripts manage critical infrastructure, every potential failure point needs explicit handling at its location, making post-mortem analysis straightforward.
104:| Component | Location | Purpose |
107:| `buv_scope_sentinel` | kindle (last, before KINDLED) | Catch undeclared `PREFIX_*` variables |
125:| Function             | Location       | First Line                                         | Can Source?       | Can use buc_step? | Purpose                                          |
181:Placement is load-bearing. shellcheck honors file-scope directives only when they precede every executable statement; placing the directive after `set -euo pipefail` or `Z«PREFIX»_SOURCED=1` reduces it to next-statement scope and the disable becomes silently inert — warnings continue to fire. The `set -u` clause of `set -euo pipefail` is the runtime backstop for the same typo class SC2153 catches statically: misspelled cross-module references die at first reference rather than silently expanding to empty.
197:1. **Resolve the moorings location.** The consumer's configuration tree (launchers, regimes) lives under a moorings directory whose path the trampoline holds as a single hardcoded literal. This is the single point of customization at the tabtarget layer — the parallel to BURC at the CLI layer. Move the moorings, edit one line; every tabtarget follows.
200:**The launcher name.** `BURD_LAUNCHER` is a bare launcher basename — `launcher.<id>_workbench.sh`. Every launcher co-locates in the moorings launcher directory (`rbml_launchers/`), so the trampoline resolves the basename directly against that one directory and exec's it, forwarding the tabtarget basename and user args unchanged. `BURD_LAUNCHER` is not a private positional convention: it is a regime variable — declared in `burd_regime`, allowlisted in `bud_dispatch` — so the launcher identity travels the same validated `BURD_*` channel as every other dispatch value, and flows downstream unchanged.
489:# From file using builtin $(<file) - NOT command substitution, a bash builtin
490:local z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP_FILE}")
493:# Never split declaration from $(<file) assignment
495:# ❌ z_content=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_FILE}")  # Unnecessary verbosity
505:local z_result=$(<"${Z«PREFIX»_TEMP2}")
532:These rules govern **command substitution** — `$(command)`, which spawns a subshell, runs a command, and can fail. They do not apply to `$(<file)`, which despite sharing the `$()` syntax is a bash **builtin file redirect**: no subshell, no command, nothing to fail. `$(<file)` needs no `|| buc_die` guard — it takes content validation (`test -n` on the result) instead, since a successful read of an empty or missing file still yields the empty string.
548:local -r z_timestamp=$(<"${Z_MODULE_TEMP1}")
616:local -r z_content=$(<"${z_file}")    # $(<file) is a builtin — safe on one line
657:  readonly Z«PREFIX»_REGISTRY_HOST="${RBGD_LOCATION}${RBGC_HOST_SUFFIX}"
748:| Predicate | `[z]«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `z«prefix»_sentinel` | 0=true, 1=false              | No (use buc_log_«source») | Never dies, status only             |
753:| Tcase     | `«tc»_«name»_tcase`           | —                    | — (exit status to suite)     | No                        | Case subshell; assertions, verification |
798:#### Predicate Functions (status only, no output)
800:Predicate functions are used to make decisions, not indicating error.
803:z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate() {
816:z«prefix»_file_valid_predicate "${z_config}" || buc_warn "Config invalid, using defaults"
1000:- Pure string assembly (concatenation, marker stamping)
1016:- **_predicate**: Need true/false for conditional logic without dying
1020:- **_tcase**: Test case verification function inside `_tcase` subshell
1028:| Element                      | Pattern                      | Example                      | Location | Name/ Case Constraints        |
1038:| Predicate Internal functions | `z«prefix»_«name»_predicate` | `zrbv_file_exists_predicate` | Impl     | snake_case                    |
1084:Comments are ONLY for code clarification that doesn't matter at runtime.
1118:# ✅ Use exit-status predicates instead
1173:Inside the subshell, `buc_die` calls `exit` which terminates only the subshell — producing a non-zero exit status that the outer `|| buc_die` or `|| z_status=$?` catches. Failure propagates through exit status, not through `exit` reaching the parent.
1212:cat <<EOF > "${z_file}"
1231:### ❌ Bare File Truncation
1237:# ✅ Explicit no-op makes truncation intent unambiguous
1241:Bare `> file` without a command is valid bash but reads like an accident at 3am. The `: >` form explicitly signals "I am creating or truncating this file." Shellcheck enforces this via SC2188.
1243:**Prefer immutable temp files.** BCG temp files are forensic artifacts — preserved for post-mortem debugging. If you find yourself truncating a temp file, the design likely needs a unique filename per step instead of reusing and clearing. Truncation is appropriate only for log files and other non-forensic outputs.
1288:**Interface contamination** is any change that expands the accepted input space, invocation forms, or alternative paths through an interface without explicit specification requirement.
1292:Every tolerance, alias, fallback, or alternative path multiplies the enumerated state space. The litmus test: **"How many zeroes got added to the enumerated state space because of this choice?"** If the answer isn't zero, the change requires explicit justification.
1300:5. **Documentation uses canonical form**: All documentation, examples, and `buc_doc_*` output must show the single canonical invocation — never a tolerated alternative.
1320:**BCG rule: Only `_predicate` functions may appear in `if`/`while` conditions.** All other functions must be invoked as simple commands with explicit `|| buc_die` / `|| return`. This completely prevents the suppression hazard.
1323:# ✅ Predicate in conditional — designed for this, never dies, status only

## Mounted `₢BVAAB` — theurge-fast-suite-cygwin-bringup

**Pace goal in one sentence:** Get `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` fully green on the uncontrolled Cygwin box (`cygwin@rocket`) by clearing the *last* blocker — a `cat` command-discipline violation that the kit-bash fixture (`rbtdru_kit_bash`) flags.

### Heat context
`₣BV` (rbk-10-mvp-uncontrolled-cygwin-test) proves theurge can self-qualify on a stock Windows+Cygwin box *we don't provision* — a consumer-credibility claim, deliberately distinct from the controlled-infrastructure `rbk-NN-win-*` heats. The hard part (the bash process-launch + platform boundaries) is **landed and green**: `BURD_OSTYPE` synthesized into the regime, theurge derives Cygwin bash via cygpath, cargo on the ssh PATH, manifest-path translation. The paddock's original thesis (bash exports `$OSTYPE` to native children) was **disproven** along the way; the docket records the correction. One in-scope item remains.

### The remaining work — and a wrinkle worth flagging
The docket scopes it as: repair the `cat` at `Tools/rbk/rbgo_OAuth.sh:297` (BCG eviction table → bash builtin). It's pre-existing, not introduced by this heat.

What I found when I read it: that `cat` lives inside a **`TEMP-FORENSIC ₢BBABL` block** — instrumentation owned by heat `₣BB` (rbk-13-mvp-release-qualification), explicitly marked *"REMOVE once the shape is captured and the match lands."* So this is another heat's code that `₣BV` is fixing in place. I read the docket's choice as deliberate: swap the `cat` for a builtin, **leave the forensic block intact** — its removal is `₣BB`'s call, not mine. I'll touch only the one line.

```bash
# line 297, current — inside { ... } >> /tmp/rbgo_BBABL_forensic.log 2>/dev/null || true
      cat "${ZRBGO_CURL_STDERR_FILE}" "${ZRBGO_OAUTH_RESPONSE_FILE}" 2>/dev/null
# proposed — printf recycles '%s' across both args; $(<file) is the BCG builtin redirect
      printf '%s' "$(<"${ZRBGO_CURL_STDERR_FILE}")" "$(<"${ZRBGO_OAUTH_RESPONSE_FILE}")"
```
The enclosing `{ ... } 2>/dev/null || true` already absorbs missing-file errors, so the builtin form preserves behavior. Command substitution strips trailing newlines vs. `cat`'s byte-fidelity — negligible for a forensic log slated for removal.

### Name assessment
Silks `theurge-fast-suite-cygwin-bringup` still fits — the cat repair is the bringup's final step, not a separate concern. No rename.

### Execution strategy
- **Sequential, single-line edit** — no parallelization, opus is overkill but I'm already here.
- **Verify locally first:** the kit-bash fixture is a static bash scan in the dependency-free `fast` tier, so `tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.fast.sh` reproduces and confirms the fix on macOS — no Cygwin round-trip needed to prove the violation is cleared.
- **Then the gold confirmation:** the docket's "Done" is *fast suite green over `cygwin@rocket`, kit-bash included*. That wants a foray run on rocket. I'd treat the local run as the fix-proof and offer the cygwin run as the heat's done-claim.

content.text:
The local proof is solid. Now, on skirmish — let me lay out what I found so the discussion is grounded, because skirmish has real teeth and a hard precondition.

## What skirmish actually is
It's the **"mini gauntlet"** (`rbtdrc_crucible.rs:2484`): the full depot→build→crucible chain *without* project-ID churn. Eleven fixtures: enrollment-validation → canonical-invest → onboarding-sequence (local kludge **+ cloud ordain**) → regime-validation/smoke → dockerfile-hygiene → cupel → then charge+run of **tadmor, moriah, srjcl, pluml**.

Its dependency profile is the union of everything:
- **GCP credentials** (it mantles the governor, divests/re-invests retriever + director, runs real cloud builds)
- **A container runtime** (four crucibles charge and run)
- **OPERATOR PRECONDITION — a canonical depot already levied.** The comment is explicit: *"install canonical prefixes and run `rbw-dL` by hand before this suite."* It deliberately omits the levy/unmake, so it **reuses** a standing depot and **creates no GCP project per run** — but it does **spend real cloud build / GAR**.

## The tension with this heat
`₣BV`'s scope is locked **runtime-free** — the paddock is firm that container tiers run on the WSL side, not Cygwin (Windows Docker Desktop has a bind-mount path-translation wall RBTDRX doesn't cover). So **skirmish cannot run on `cygwin@rocket`** at all. It would have to run on a runtime-capable host: `wsl@rocket` (Docker live), `cerebro`, or local — none of which is the Cygwin box this heat is about.

That makes skirmish *not* a continuation of the Cygwin bring-up — it's a heavier integration gate that lives outside `₣BV`'s scope. Which connects to the docket's recorded **known gap**: *"a whole cloud process has not been exercised from Cygwin… whether it belongs here or in a new heat is an operator call."*

## On my change specifically
The `cat` I swapped is on the OAuth token-mint **failure** branch (forensic capture when a mint fails). Skirmish exercises real OAuth mint, but a *healthy* run won't hit that branch unless it trips a propagation race. So skirmish isn't really a regression test of my one-line change — it's the natural next integration checkpoint regardless.

## What I need from you to proceed
Three things, the first gating the rest:

1. **Is a canonical depot currently levied?** Skirmish hard-requires it. I won't probe GCP (`rbw-dl`) without your say-so — do you already have one standing, or do we need the `rbw-dL` + canonical-prefix setup first?
2. **Where should it run** — `wsl@rocket`, `cerebro`, or local? (Wherever has the runtime + creds you want to spend.)
3. **What's the goal** — a broad regression gate now that the fast tier is clean, or a deliberate step at closing the "cloud-process-from-Cygwin" known gap (which would more likely be a *new* heat, since skirmish itself can't touch the Cygwin box)?

I'd also flag: I haven't wrapped `₢BVAAB`. The in-scope work is done and proven locally, but the docket's "Done" names *fast suite green over `cygwin@rocket`*. Whether you want that cygwin confirmation run before we wrap, or consider the local proof sufficient, is your call.

lastPrompt: I do want you to do the work and then run the test after.  If that goes well, we can discuss kicking off skirmish.

aiTitle: Complete work and run tests before skirmish

content.text:
Recon's back, and we hit the wall **earlier than the paddock predicted** — not at the bind-mount path-translation layer, but one step before it.

## What's there
- **Reachable, toolchain present.** Cygwin `CYGWIN_NT-10.0-26200`, `cargo` at `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/.cargo/bin/cargo`, skirmish tabtarget present.
- **Repo at `1e666ce`, clean** — that's `₢BVAAB`'s last commit, *one behind* my `cat` fix (`e6aac6887`, not yet pushed). Remote is `origin` → `github.com/bhyslop/recipemuster.git`. Irrelevant to *this* probe (the `cat` line is on the OAuth failure branch, nothing to do with the container runtime), but we'd sync before any real skirmish run.
- **Docker Desktop client 28.3.2 is installed and on the Cygwin PATH** (`/cygdrive/c/Program Files/Docker/Docker/resources/bin/docker`).

## The wall
```
error during connect: in the default daemon configuration on Windows, the docker
client must be run with elevated privileges to connect:
open //./pipe/docker_engine: Access is denied.
```

**Characterization:** This is a *daemon-authorization* wall, not a path-translation one. The error is **"Access is denied"** on `\\.\pipe\docker_engine` — not "cannot find the file" — which means the pipe **exists** (Docker Desktop's daemon is running) but the `cygwin` Windows account's logon token **isn't authorized** for it. The standard cause: the ad-hoc `cygwin` account (deliberately independent of the formal garrison — "safe scratch") was never added to the **`docker-users`** local group, which is the group Docker Desktop grants pipe access to. So skirmish can't even reach `docker build`/`docker run`; the bind-mount path wall is still downstream and untested.

This is consistent with the existing design: `wsl@rocket` is the documented "Docker daemon live" host. The cygwin account simply has no daemon rights.

## One repair I'd attempt
Add the `cygwin` Windows account to `docker-users` via the admin channel, then reconnect (group membership is baked into the logon token at ssh-connect, so the *current* sessions won't see it — a fresh ssh login will):
```
tt/buw-jpS bujn-winpc 'net localgroup docker-users cygwin /add'
```
Then re-probe `docker version` over a fresh `ssh cygwin@rocket`. If the pipe ACL grants `docker-users` (the default), that clears this wall and we find out whether the *next* wall — the bind-mount path translation the paddock actually worried about — is where it really dies.

**I'm stopping here to confirm** rather than just doing it: this changes a privilege-group membership on your Windows box (grants the scratch `cygwin` account Docker daemon access), and it needs a session restart to take effect, so it's not a clean one-shot. Two small residual unknowns: I'm inferring the daemon is up from the "Access is denied" (vs. "file not found") signature, and I'm assuming Docker Desktop's pipe ACL grants `docker-users` (its default).

Want me to run the group-add via the admin channel and re-probe? Or would you rather handle the Windows-side privilege change yourself and have me pick back up at the re-probe?

This is exactly the "simple administrative action" you authorized — adding the scratch account to the group Docker Desktop already uses.

content.text:
The wall is cleared. `cygwin` is now in `docker-users` (alongside `bhyslop`), and a fresh login reaches the daemon: `docker version` returns full **Server** info (daemon up, `linux/amd64` — Docker Desktop is in its WSL2/Linux-container backend) and `docker ps` works (one container up). That's the simple, reasonable-dev fix done and verified.

A note before I launch skirmish, and one decision I made on your behalf:

- **Running at `1e666ce`, not my `cat` fix.** Rocket's clone is clean at `₢BVAAB`'s last commit; my `e6aac6887` is local/unpushed. I checked skirmish's fixture set — it does **not** include the kit-bash fixture, so the `cat` change is never exercised by skirmish. Running at `1e666ce` is behaviorally identical for this probe, so I'm *not* pushing to the public remote just for this. We sync later.
- **What skirmish will spend:** it re-mantles the governor and divests/re-invests director+retriever (real IAM churn, no $), then local kludge (`docker build`) + cloud ordain (real Cloud Build $) before the four crucibles charge. My prediction: the **bind-mount / docker path-translation wall** is the next failure, and it likely hits at the *local kludge `docker build`* step — which is **before** the expensive cloud build. So this should be an informative, cheap-ish failure if my read is right.

Launching skirmish in the background so it survives a long run; I'll be notified when it exits and report where it landed.

The stdout buffer only carries pass/fail lines; theurge writes case detail to the trace dir. Let me fetch the actual error from rocket.

content.text:
Found it — and it's **not** a Cygwin or Docker wall at all:

```
FAILED: rbtdrk_governor_mantle
precondition 'canonical depot moniker installed' not met:
  BURS_TINCTURE not in .../station-files/burs.env
remediation: rerun rbtdrk_depot_levy or the full canonical-establish fixture
```

This is skirmish's documented **operator precondition** — the suite comment said *"install canonical prefixes and run `rbw-dL` by hand before this suite."* A depot being alive in GCP isn't sufficient; the **local station config on `cygwin@rocket` doesn't have the canonical depot moniker installed** (`BURS_TINCTURE` is absent from `burs.env`). So canonical-invest can't even start — and we never reach the container steps where the actual Cygwin/Docker path question lives.

Let me confirm the local station state and find what "install canonical prefixes" concretely requires, so I can tell you exactly what's missing rather than guess.

input.command: ssh -o ConnectTimeout=15 -o ControlMaster=no -o ControlPath=none cygwin@rocket 'SF=~/projects/station-files; echo "=LS station-files="; ls -la "$SF" 2>&1; echo; echo "=burs.env="; cat "$SF"/burs.env 2>&1'

aiTitle: Complete work and run tests before skirmish

content.text:
Wait — that's a surprise worth chasing. The `burs.env` on rocket **does contain `BURS_TINCTURE=a`**, yet theurge reported it "not in" the file. The file has the key; the read failed anyway. That smells like a real Cygwin issue, not a missing precondition. Note the path in the error: `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/projects/.../burs.env` — and theurge is a **Windows-native binary**, which can't open `/cygdrive/...` paths through Windows file APIs.

Let me read the Rust that does this read to confirm whether it routes through RBTDRX path translation or reads the raw Cygwin path directly.
[Request interrupted by user]

message.content: Check the cygwin burs.env; it is quite possible that is an old version

content.text: Good call — let me verify the file empirically rather than trust my path arithmetic. I'd reasoned theurge's error path normalizes to the same file I catted, but you have context I don't (the station file may have been regenerated/moved/schema-changed). Let me check the exact bytes, hunt for duplicate copies, and confirm what `BURD_STATION_FILE` actually resolves to.

input.command: ssh -o ConnectTimeout=15 -o ControlMaster=no -o ControlPath=none cygwin@rocket 'echo "=FIND burs.env under ~/projects="; find ~/projects -name "burs.env" 2>/dev/null; echo; echo "=od -c (exact bytes)="; od -c ~/projects/station-files/burs.env 2>&1; echo "=STAT="; stat ~/projects/station-files/burs.env 2>&1 | head -8'

content.text:
Decisive — and it **disproves the old-version theory**, while pinning the real cause. Three facts settle it:

1. **Single, current, well-formed file.** Only one `burs.env` exists on the box (`~/projects/station-files/burs.env`, no stale duplicate). `od -c` shows clean LF bytes, no CRLF, no BOM: `BURS_LOG_DIR=../logs-buk\n BURS_USER=cygwin\n BURS_TINCTURE=a\n`. The key **is** there and well-formed — if theurge had *opened* the file, `rbtdrk_read_env_value` would return `"a"` (and Rust's `.lines()` strips `\r` anyway, so CRLF wouldn't have mattered either).

2. **The path theurge was handed is Cygwin-form.** `bul_launcher.sh:57` sets `BURD_STATION_FILE="${ZBUL_PROJECT_ROOT}/${BURC_STATION_FILE}"`, and `ZBUL_PROJECT_ROOT` is a POSIX path — so at runtime `BURD_STATION_FILE` = `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/projects/.../burs.env`, exactly the path in the error.

3. **theurge reads it raw, with no RBTDRX translation.** `rbtdrk_burs_tincture()` → `rbtdrk_read_env_value()` → `std::fs::read_to_string(path)` (`rbtdrk_canonical.rs:144`), passing the Cygwin path straight to a **Windows-native binary**. Windows file APIs can't resolve `/cygdrive/c/...`, so the open fails → `.ok()?` → `None` → "BURS_TINCTURE not in …".

## The actual wall
This is a **fourth path-translation boundary** — a sibling to the three the heat already cleared (`.sh`-launch, cargo `--manifest-path`, shellcheck-arg). Its signature: **theurge's native-Rust code reads a config file via `std::fs` directly from a Cygwin-form path, bypassing RBTDRX.** It is *not* the Docker/bind-mount wall (still downstream, untested) and *not* a stale station file.

And it's not a one-off. The same raw-read pattern lives in `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:147` and `rbtdrk_read_env_value` is reused for `rbrr.env`/`rbrd.env` reads too. **Every** canonical/pristine/crucible fixture does native-Rust config reads → the whole service/crucible/skirmish/gauntlet family is blocked on Cygwin at this layer. This is exactly why only the *fast* tier ever went green: its validation fixtures run through **bash subprocesses** (which read files in Cygwin bash, no translation needed), while canonical-invest is the first fixture to read a config file from native Rust.

**Repair shape** (if we pursue it): nativize `BURD_STATION_FILE` (posix→Windows via RBTDRX's existing cygpath, the same machinery `rbtdri_bash_program` uses) before the `std::fs` read — ideally centralized in `rbtdrk_read_env_value`/the shared helper so the pristine site and the rbrr/rbrd reads are covered in one stroke.

## Where this leaves us — your call
We've answered "how far does Cygwin+Docker get skirmish, and where does it stop": it stops *before* anything Docker-related, at theurge's native config read. So the **Docker question is still unanswered**, gated behind this path-translation work (and possibly more native-read sites after it).

This is genuine new code work, **beyond the `cat`-repair pace I mounted**, and it cuts straight against this heat's locked runtime-free scope. So:

- **Chase it now** — I implement the RBTDRX nativization in the shared read helper, rebuild on rocket, re-run skirmish, and we find the *next* wall (likely Docker bind-mounts). Deliberate scope expansion on `₣BV`.
- **Record and stop** — capture this as a precise finding (new pace under `₣BV`, or an itch/follow-on heat for "theurge native-read path-translation"), leaving the runtime-free lock intact. The `cat` fix stays the in-scope deliverable.

Which way do you want to go?

message.content: You used a ton of words and I still can't tell what is the issue and if you understand it.  State clearly.

content.text:
**The issue, plainly:**

theurge is a Windows program. It was handed a Cygwin-style path (`/cygdrive/c/...`) for `burs.env`. Windows can't open `/cygdrive/...` paths — only Cygwin can. So theurge failed to open the file and reported "BURS_TINCTURE not in burs.env."

**The file is fine.** It has `BURS_TINCTURE=a`. theurge just never read it.

**Why it matters:** skirmish died here, long before Docker. So we still don't know if Docker works on Cygwin — this wall is in front of it.

**Fix:** translate the path to Windows form (`C:\...`) before theurge reads it.

Do you want me to make that fix and re-run, or stop here?

message.content: Much better.  Lets discuss cleanest yet multiplatform approach to this.  Thoughts?

content.text:
Good — this is the right thing to slow down on. Here's how I see it.

**The principle**

theurge has two kinds of path consumer, and they want *opposite* forms:
- **bash / `.sh` / POSIX tools** want `/cygdrive/...` — RBTDRX already converts native→posix for these.
- **theurge's own `std::fs`** wants `C:\...` — nothing converts posix→native. That's the entire gap.

So the fix isn't "translate paths" broadly; it's "add the one missing direction, and apply it only where a Cygwin path crosses into native Rust."

**Precedent that settles the shape**

The prior pace hit the mirror image of this and already chose the answer: it *dropped* `BURD_BASH_BIN` (a regime var that carried a pre-nativized bash path) and instead had theurge nativize `/bin/bash` itself via cygpath. Lesson: **don't make the regime carry per-consumer path variants — convert at theurge's edge.** That rules out the tempting "have the launcher export a Windows-form station path" — it can't, because bash `source`s the same `BURD_STATION_FILE` and needs the POSIX form. One var, two consumers, so theurge must convert internally.

The building block already exists: RBTDRX *already* does posix→native via cygpath when it nativizes `/bin/bash`. We just generalize that.

**Cleanest approach**

1. **Generalize the existing bash-path nativization into a reusable `rbtdrx_posix_to_native(p)`** — identity off-Cygwin (one branch, zero behavior change on Linux/macOS), cygpath on Cygwin. It's the symmetric twin of `native_to_posix`.
2. **Translate at the door, once** — at the point a Cygwin path enters native Rust, i.e. when read from the environment. A single helper like `path_from_env("BURD_STATION_FILE")` = `var()` + `posix_to_native`. Downstream `.join()` and every `std::fs` call are then native for free. **Not** at each `read_to_string` — that's contamination, and you'll miss one.

**The one real choice for us — how far to push the membrane now**

- **Minimal**: nativize just the station-file ingestion (canonical + its pristine twin), rebuild on rocket, re-run skirmish, find the next wall.
- **Systematic**: funnel *all* env-sourced paths through the one helper now (covers the `rbrr`/`rbrd` reads and `root`), maybe behind a typed `NativePath` so a future raw `std::fs` can't silently regress.

I lean **minimal-first**: this is still an experiment, the Docker bind-mount wall is untested behind this one, and each wall cleared may expose another. Systematic hardening earns its keep once Cygwin graduates from experiment to supported.

**One thing I'd check before writing it:** where `root` comes from — if it's native `current_dir()` it's already fine; if it's an env var it needs the same funnel. That determines whether "minimal" is truly just the station file or a touch more.

What's your instinct on minimal vs. systematic — and do you agree theurge-side conversion is the right home, versus something at the launcher I might be undervaluing?

content.text:
Yes — **theurge-only.** Launcher and regime stay untouched; convert at theurge's edge. That's the prior pace's precedent and it holds.

On **`root`: not ambiguous as a concept** — the only open question is *representation*:
- if theurge gets it from `current_dir()` → already native (Windows tracks cwd in native form) → nothing to do.
- if from a launcher-set env var → POSIX → needs the funnel.

The earlier error proved the *launcher's* root (`ZBUL_PROJECT_ROOT`) is POSIX, but theurge's `root` may be sourced independently. That's a grep, not a debate.

On **systematic — I'm on board, with one scoping line and one concern:**

- **Scope it as two tiers so "systematic" doesn't silently balloon.** Tier 1 = the *funnel*: every path read from env/argv goes through one `posix_to_native`. Tier 2 = a typed `NativePath` newtype that makes a future raw `std::fs` a *compile error*. I'd do **Tier 1 now, defer Tier 2** — the newtype is a signature-touching refactor I wouldn't spend while Docker downstream is still unproven. Say the word if you want the newtype too.
- **The concern actually makes it cleaner.** Systematic forces one invariant: **native is theurge's internal canonical form; POSIX is produced only at the bash door — which RBTDRX already does.** More principled than minimal. The one risk it surfaces: if any bash-bound path today rides on "already POSIX from the env" *without* going through RBTDRX, nativizing ingestion would break it. So systematic needs an audit that every bash-crossing renders via RBTDRX. Better to find that now.

Let me run that audit now — it's read-only and settles `root` definitively plus gives us the site list to scope Tier 1.

aiTitle: Complete work and run tests before skirmish

content.text:
Audit's back, and it **reshapes the plan in our favor**. Three findings:

**1. `rbtdrx_posix_to_native` already exists** (`rbtdrx_platform.rs:63`) — identity off-Cygwin (line 82), `/cygdrive` string fast-path (`rbtdrx_cygdrive_to_native`), `cygpath -w` fallback, with a unit-test seam (`_for(s, is_cygwin)`). So "systematic" needs **zero new RBTDRX code** — just *calls* to the funnel that's already built and tested.

**2. `root` is `std::env::current_dir()` — everywhere, no exceptions** (dogfight, cupel, pristine, onboarding, main, fast all source it that way). On a Windows-native binary, `current_dir()` returns `C:\...` (Windows tracks cwd natively). So **`root` is already native — it needs nothing.** Not ambiguous: your "root is always the cwd" + the fact that a native binary's cwd is native settles it. Empirically confirmed too — the fast tier passed on Cygwin, and it runs `root`-relative bash; if `root` were POSIX-broken it would've failed. (Same reason the trace dir in the skirmish output printed as `C:\Users\cygwin\...\temp-buk` — native, root-derived — and writes succeeded.)

**3. The POSIX leak surface is tiny.** Of 291 `std::fs` sites across 17 files, nearly all are `root.join(...)` → already native. The *only* POSIX ingress is direct `env::var` reads of `BURD_*` **path** vars. Those are just:
- `rbtdrk_canonical.rs:117` — `BURD_STATION_FILE` ← **the bug**
- `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:153` — `BURD_STATION_FILE` ← its twin
- two maybes I'm about to pin down: `rbtdth_helpers.rs:32` (`BURD_TEMP_DIR`) and `main.rs:63` (a generic getter)

So systematic is nearly as small as minimal, but fixes the whole *class*: **one shared `path_from_env(var)` = `env::var` + `rbtdrx_posix_to_native`, applied at the ~2–4 doors. `root` untouched.** And given only a handful of doors exist, the Tier-2 typed-`NativePath` newtype now looks like over-engineering — I'd **skip it**, not just defer.

Let me pin the exact door count before proposing edits.

aiTitle: Complete work and run tests before skirmish

content.text:
Doors pinned. The inventory is even cleaner than I hoped — and it turns out **theurge already has the pattern**:

- `main.rs:63` (`rbtdb_read_dispatch_dir`) — **already funnels**: `Ok(v) => rbtdrx_posix_to_native(&v)`. This is the precedent.
- `rbtdrk_canonical.rs:117` (`BURD_STATION_FILE`) — `PathBuf::from(path_str)`, **not funneled** → the bug.
- `rbtdrp_pristine.rs:153` (`BURD_STATION_FILE`) — same, **not funneled** → the twin.
- `rbtdth_helpers.rs:32` (`BURD_TEMP_DIR`) — `PathBuf::from(v)`, not funneled, but **test-only** (panics unless launched via `tt/rbw-tt.Test.sh`; off the skirmish path).

So the entire class is **two production sites that diverged from the pattern `main.rs` already follows**, plus one test-side sibling. That's the whole leak.

## Recommendation (systematic, but small and principled)

Promote the membrane into one place instead of three copies:

1. **Add `rbtdrx_path_from_env(var) -> Result<PathBuf,String>`** to RBTDRX = `env::var` + `rbtdrx_posix_to_native`. RBTDRX already owns the conversion; now it owns the ingestion too — *one* door for the whole binary.
2. **Route all three readers through it** — `main.rs` (replacing its inline copy), `rbtdrk_canonical`, `rbtdrp_pristine`. Fix the test helper too for completeness.
3. **Leave `root`/`current_dir()` untouched**, with a one-line comment: *native on Cygwin — do not nativize* (so nobody double-converts later).

This fixes the *class*, unifies the membrane, and is ~one new function + 3 call-site edits. The Tier-2 `NativePath` newtype isn't worth it — with a single ingestion funnel and `root` structurally native, there are no other doors to leak through.

**Invariant we're locking in:** native is theurge's internal form; POSIX is produced only at the bash door (existing `rbtdrx_native_to_posix`). The station-file path is Rust-only, never crosses to bash, so this change can't disturb the bash direction.

Want me to implement it this way, rebuild on rocket, and re-run skirmish to find the next wall?

content.content:

13	// limitations under the License.
14	//
15	// Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
16	//
17	// RBTDRX — cross-platform path transmutation at theurge's bash/Rust boundary.
18	//
19	// Cygwin runs Windows-native binaries from a POSIX-style shell. Paths cross
20	// the boundary in both directions:
21	//
22	//   - Intake (POSIX → native): env vars and arguments arrive as
23	//     `/cygdrive/c/...` or bare `/home/...` from Cygwin bash; Rust's PathBuf
24	//     joins them with backslashes on a Windows target, producing mixed
25	//     `/cygdrive/c/...\rbtd` paths Windows resolves unpredictably.
26	//   - Outflow (native → POSIX): PathBuf::display() emits native form
27	//     (`C:\foo\bar`); embedded in a bash script or env var consumed by
28	//     Cygwin bash, the consumer sees an unparseable path.
29	//
30	// This module provides the two conversions plus a cached Cygwin-detection
31	// probe. On Linux and macOS both conversions are identity, so call sites
32	// can be unconditional.
33	
34	use std::path::{Path, PathBuf};
35	use std::process::Command;
36	use std::sync::OnceLock;
37	
38	static RBTDRX_IS_CYGWIN: OnceLock<bool> = OnceLock::new();
39	
40	/// Dispatch-synthesized BURD member carrying bash's OSTYPE across the process
41	/// boundary. bash's own `$OSTYPE` is a shell variable, not exported, so a
42	/// native binary never inherits it; `bud_dispatch.sh` reads the live `$OSTYPE`
43	/// in bash and re-exports it under this name.
44	const RBTDRX_ENV_OSTYPE: &str = "BURD_OSTYPE";
45	
46	/// Detect whether we're running on Cygwin.
47	///
48	/// Reads the dispatch-provided `BURD_OSTYPE` (see `RBTDRX_ENV_OSTYPE`) rather
49	/// than bash's own `$OSTYPE`, which a native binary does not inherit. Result is
50	/// cached for binary lifetime.
51	pub fn rbtdrx_is_cygwin() -> bool {
52	    *RBTDRX_IS_CYGWIN.get_or_init(|| {
53	        matches!(std::env::var(RBTDRX_ENV_OSTYPE).as_deref(), Ok("cygwin"))
54	    })
55	}
56	
57	/// Convert a path string from POSIX form (as Cygwin bash uses) to native
58	/// PathBuf form (as the Windows OS uses). On non-Cygwin platforms, identity.
59	///
60	/// Returns `Err` only when running on Cygwin AND the path is not a recognised
61	/// fast-path shape AND the `cygpath` fallback fails — i.e., the platform
62	/// claims Cygwin via OSTYPE but cygpath is unreachable.
63	pub fn rbtdrx_posix_to_native(s: &str) -> Result<PathBuf, String> {
64	    rbtdrx_posix_to_native_for(s, rbtdrx_is_cygwin())
65	}
66	
67	/// Convert a Path from native form to POSIX form suitable for embedding in
68	/// a Cygwin bash script or for passing as an env var to a Cygwin-bash
69	/// tabtarget. On non-Cygwin platforms, returns the path's display form.
70	///
71	/// Best-effort: on Cygwin, falls back to `cygpath -u` for unrecognised
72	/// native shapes; if cygpath fails the original display form is returned
73	/// rather than failing the caller. Outflow sites (env vars, format!
74	/// interpolations) cannot meaningfully propagate errors mid-script.
75	pub fn rbtdrx_native_to_posix(p: &Path) -> String {
76	    rbtdrx_native_to_posix_for(&p.to_string_lossy(), rbtdrx_is_cygwin())
77	}
78	
79	// ── Pure implementation — explicit is_cygwin parameter for unit tests ──
80	
81	pub(crate) fn rbtdrx_posix_to_native_for(s: &str, is_cygwin: bool) -> Result<PathBuf, String> {
82	    if !is_cygwin {
83	        return Ok(PathBuf::from(s));
84	    }
85	
86	    // Fast path: /cygdrive/X/foo → X:\foo
87	    if let Some(native) = rbtdrx_cygdrive_to_native(s) {
88	        return Ok(native);
89	    }
90	
91	    // Already native Windows form (drive-letter prefix)
92	    if rbtdrx_looks_native_windows(s) {
93	        return Ok(PathBuf::from(s));
94	    }
95	
96	    // Slow path: bare POSIX (/home/..., /tmp/...) needs cygpath
97	    rbtdrx_cygpath(s, "-w")
98	        .map(PathBuf::from)
99	        .ok_or_else(|| format!("rbtdrx: cygpath -w failed for '{}'", s))
100	}

89c208f jjb:1015-e6aac6887:₣BV:n: Nativize theurge's env-sourced config-file paths so the Windows-native binary (x86_64-pc-windows-gnu under Cygwin) can open them. theurge read BURD_STATION_FILE — exported by bul_launcher.sh in Cygwin /cygdrive POSIX form — straight into std::fs::read_to_string with no path translation, so on Cygwin the read silently failed and canonical-invest's governor_mantle precondition reported 'BURS_TINCTURE not in burs.env' on a file that contained the key. This blocked the entire canonical/pristine/crucible/skirmish fixture family; only the fast tier ran green because its validation fixtures read files via bash subprocesses, not native Rust. Added rbtdrx_path_from_env() as theurge's single env-path intake membrane (env::var + existing rbtdrx_posix_to_native; identity off-Cygwin). Routed the two station-file readers (rbtdrk_burs_tincture, rbtdrp_burs_tincture), main.rs's dispatch-dir reader (deleting its now-redundant rbtdb_read_dispatch_dir copy), and the rbtdth test scratch-root helper through it. root stays native (current_dir() is native on Cygwin; left untouched, documented). Invariant locked: native is theurge's internal form, POSIX produced only at the bash door via existing rbtdrx_native_to_posix. Local theurge build + 137 unit tests green on macOS (identity path).

- Live depot on rocket: `canest3bhm100001` → tincture **`b`**
- Rocket's `burs.env`: `BURS_TINCTURE=a` → fixture expects `canest3a…`

The station file is one era behind the live depot — exactly the staleness you suspected, just hidden until my fix let theurge read the file. (The comment even records the `canest2`→`canest3` era bumps; this is the same kind of drift, `a`→`b`.)

- **Cygwin path-translation: solved and proven.** theurge now reads `burs.env` and `rbrd.env` natively on Cygwin. That was the code question for this heat, and it's answered.
- **Remaining blocker is pure config**, no code, no GCP spend to fix: rocket's `BURS_TINCTURE` says `a`, the live canonical depot is `b`.

If `canest3bhm100001` is the canonical depot you want dogfight to use, the fix is editing rocket's `~/projects/station-files/burs.env`: `BURS_TINCTURE=a` → `BURS_TINCTURE=b`. No relevy, no cloud cost. Then re-run dogfight.

content.text:
**The precondition cleared — the tincture fix worked.** The failure mode advanced again: it's no longer a precondition check, it's the *actual* governor-mantle operation running on Cygwin and reaching Payor authentication:

```
rbgp_governor_mantle: Authenticate as Payor
ERROR: OAuth credentials expired or invalid - run rbgp_payor_oauth_refresh: reauth related error (invalid_rapt)
ERROR: Failed to exchange OAuth refresh token
ERROR: Failed to authenticate as Payor via OAuth
```

This is **not Cygwin and not code** — the operation validated its inputs, ran through all the path/translation layers cleanly, and got a clean verdict from Google: the **Payor OAuth credential is expired** (`invalid_rapt` = Google's reauth-proof-token expiry — it demands periodic *interactive* reauth for sensitive admin ops like SA mantling). This is the Pale: Google's auth policy plus a stale credential.

## Where the experiment landed

We walked the dogfight chain on Cygwin through four walls:
1. ✅ Docker daemon access (added `cygwin` to `docker-users`)
2. ✅ theurge native config-read path translation — **the real code bug; fixed and committed (`89c208f`)**
3. ✅ Stale station tincture (`a`→`bhm`)
4. ⛔ Expired Payor OAuth credential — routine operator maintenance, **not** a platform issue

The heat's actual code question is **answered and shipped**: theurge reads config files natively on Cygwin, and the canonical-invest machinery executes correctly there. (The original Docker *bind-mount* question is still untouched — dogfight is auth-gated before its cloud steps, and charges no crucible anyway. But the daemon-access half is proven.)

## Decision — your call
Remediation is `rbw-gPR` (PayorRefresh) / `rbgp_payor_oauth_refresh`. But `invalid_rapt` means Google wants **interactive** reauth — I can't complete that over a non-interactive ssh; it needs you to run the refresh (browser flow).

So: refresh the Payor cred when you can and tell me to re-run dogfight, or call the experiment here. Given the time constraint, this is a clean stopping point — the code deliverable is done and pushed; the rest is credential maintenance. (Whenever you want, the paddock's owed curry could capture this four-wall map and the native-read fix.)

content.text:
Yes — that's the **`tadmor` suite** (`tt/rbw-ts.TestSuite.tadmor.sh`), and it's exactly the shape you describe. From its definition in `rbtdrc_crucible.rs`:

> *"Tadmor self-contained — fully local, no GCP/depot/project."*

Two fixtures in sequence, nothing else:
1. **`kludge-tadmor`** — builds **both** vessels (sentry + bottle) locally via `docker build` and commits each hallmark into the nameplate (the fixture owns the notch).
2. **`tadmor`** crucible — charges the containers against that nameplate, runs the ~34 tadmor-security cases inside the bottle, quenches.

**This is actually the *best* suite to answer the original Docker-on-Cygwin question** — better than dogfight. It's the only fully-local path that both **builds** (`docker build` → build-context paths) and **charges a crucible** (`docker run`/compose with **bind-mounts** → the exact path-translation wall the paddock worried about). No payor, no OAuth, no canonical-invest, no cloud — so none of the walls we've been clearing today are in the way. It goes straight at Docker.

Two things to note before running it:
- **Sequential only** — I can't launch it while dogfight is still running (shared regime/container namespaces; the CLAUDE.md is explicit: never run fixtures in parallel). So it waits for dogfight to finish.
- **It commits hallmarks on rocket** — kludge-tadmor notches into rocket's local clone, so rocket's `main` will gain local hallmark commits diverging from origin. Fine for a test box, just flagging it.

Want me to queue it up to run on `cygwin@rocket` as soon as dogfight completes?

content.text:
Dogfight landed with a **major milestone and a precisely-located new wall.**

## The win
**The entire canonical-invest chain passed on uncontrolled Cygwin** — `governor_mantle` ✅, `retriever_invest` ✅, `director_invest` ✅. That's real GCP IAM (governor mantle, SA invest, access probes, propagation waits) driven from the Cygwin box. Combined with today's native-read fix, theurge's whole service-tier machinery now works there. That's well past anything this heat had proven.

## The new wall — and it's the Docker one
The dogfight fixture failed at its first step, `ordain` (the conjure cloud-build), specifically at the local **docker build of the context image**:

```
rbfd_ordain Building context image for rbev-busybox
ERROR: failed to build: resolve : CreateFile \cygdrive\c\Users\cygwin\projects\temp-buk\temp-...: The system cannot find the file specified.
```

This is the **Docker path-translation wall the paddock predicted** — now empirically hit and located. A foundry bash script (`rbfd_ordain`) passed a Cygwin `/cygdrive/c/...` context path to **Windows `docker.exe`**, which normalized the slashes to `\cygdrive\c\...` but never drive-translated it, so `CreateFile` failed (it needs `C:\...`).

Crucially, this is a **different boundary** from the one I fixed today:
- Today's fix: **theurge's native Rust** reads (RBTDRX-owned). ✅ done.
- This wall: **foundry bash → Windows `docker.exe`** path arguments. RBTDRX doesn't reach here — it's a separate surface in the `rbf*`/`rbfd_ordain` (and `rbj*` charge) bash. Fix shape: `cygpath -w` the context/mount paths before handing them to docker.

(Also seen, non-fatal: a build-quota *warning* — fresh depot, 2 concurrent vs 3 needed. Didn't cause this failure, but a full run would eventually need `rbw-gq`.)

## On tadmor — same wall, cleaner reproduction
The `tadmor` suite would hit **this exact class of wall** at its `kludge` step (also a local `docker build`), with **no GCP chain in front of it** — so it'd reproduce the Cygwin→Windows-docker path boundary faster and in isolation, likely at the very first vessel build. That makes it a good way to *pin* the local docker-path wall precisely (and see whether kludge fails at the identical site or a sibling).

So, your call given the clock:
- **Run tadmor** — isolates and confirms the docker build-context path wall locally, no cloud, ~quick fail. Good characterization.
- **Stop here** — we've already located the wall and its fix domain (foundry bash, `cygpath -w` before docker). Clean stopping point.

Which way?

file.content:
# The pouch carries the Dockerfile and supporting files that GCB
# needs in /workspace during the build.
#
# Args: token  sigil  build_context_path  hallmark
# Side-effect: writes context image tag to ${ZRBFD_CONTEXT_PREFIX}tag.txt
zrbfd_push_build_context() {
  zrbfd_sentinel

  local -r z_token="$1"
  local -r z_sigil="$2"
  local -r z_bldctx="$3"
  local -r z_hallmark="$4"

  test -d "${z_bldctx}" || buc_die "Build context directory not found: ${z_bldctx}"
  test -n "${z_hallmark}" || buc_die "Hallmark required for pouch tag"

  local -r z_gar_host="${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_HOST}"
  local -r z_context_tag_file="${ZRBFD_CONTEXT_PREFIX}tag.txt"
  local -r z_context_dockerfile="${ZRBFD_CONTEXT_PREFIX}Dockerfile"

  local -r z_context_tag="${z_gar_host}/${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_PATH}/${RBGL_HALLMARKS_ROOT}/${z_hallmark}/pouch:${z_hallmark}"

  # Build FROM SCRATCH image containing build context
  buc_step "Building context image for ${z_sigil}"
  printf 'FROM scratch\nCOPY . /build-context/\n' > "${z_context_dockerfile}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to write context Dockerfile"

  docker build --platform "${RBGC_BUILD_RUNNER_PLATFORM}" -f "${z_context_dockerfile}" -t "${z_context_tag}" "${z_bldctx}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to build context image"

  # Push to GAR
  buc_step "Pushing context image to GAR"
  rbgo_docker_login "${z_token}" "${z_gar_host}"

  docker push "${z_context_tag}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to push context image to GAR"

  echo "${z_context_tag}" > "${z_context_tag_file}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to persist context image tag"

  buc_info "Context image pushed: ${z_context_tag}"
}


######################################################################

content.text:
Not compose — it's a bare `docker build`. The exact call is `rbfd_FoundryDirectorBuild.sh:853`:

```bash
docker build --platform "${RBGC_BUILD_RUNNER_PLATFORM}" \
  -f "${z_context_dockerfile}" \
  -t "${z_context_tag}" \
  "${z_bldctx}"          # ← build-context positional
```

It's the "pouch" step: it writes a generated `FROM scratch` / `COPY . /build-context/` Dockerfile, then builds it to package the whole build context into an image to ship to Cloud Build.

**The failing argument is the build-context positional `${z_bldctx}`** — and it's verbatim the path in the error: `/cygdrive/c/Users/cygwin/projects/temp-buk/temp-20260602-131054-551-102`. Windows `docker.exe`/BuildKit's frontend tried to *resolve* that context dir, slash-normalized `/cygdrive/c/...` → `\cygdrive\c\...`, and `CreateFile` failed because it never drive-translated the cygdrive prefix (it needs `C:\...`). That's the `resolve : CreateFile \cygdrive\c\...` signature.

On your compose hunch: that's the right instinct, and exactly why it *didn't* save us here — `docker compose` does carry some Windows path normalization, but plain `docker build` hands the context straight to BuildKit with no `/cygdrive` awareness. The `-f` Dockerfile arg (`${z_context_dockerfile}`, also a `/cygdrive` temp path) would hit the same wall on the very next breath; the positional just resolves first.

So the boundary is concrete: **foundry bash passing `/cygdrive` paths as `docker build` arguments to Windows `docker.exe`**. The fix is explicit `cygpath -w` on the path args (the `-f` and the context positional, gated on Cygwin) — RBTDRX doesn't reach here because this is the `rbf*`/`rbfd` bash→docker boundary, not theurge's Rust↔bash crossing. And it's not a one-off: every foundry/crucible `docker build`/`docker run -v`/mount that takes a path arg is in the same class. `tadmor`'s kludge step is the same `docker build` pattern, so it'd reproduce this immediately — which is what makes it the clean local probe if you want to pin the full set of docker path sites without the GCP chain.

local -r z_vouch_ref="${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_HOST}/${ZRBFC_REGISTRY_PATH}/${RBGL_HALLMARKS_ROOT}/${z_hallmark}/${RBGC_ARK_BASENAME_VOUCH}:${z_hallmark}"

  buc_step "Kludge build: ${RBRV_SIGIL}"
  buc_info "Hallmark: ${z_hallmark}"
  buc_info "Image tag: ${z_image_ref}"

  # Build locally (host platform only — no multi-arch for dev builds).
  # Buildx defaults to not re-pulling base images; the presence guard above has
  # already verified every slot is in the local cache, so the build never networks.
  buc_step "Building image locally"
  docker build \
    "${z_build_args[@]}" \
    -f "${RBRV_CONJURE_DOCKERFILE}" \
    -t "${z_image_ref}" \
    "${RBRV_CONJURE_BLDCONTEXT}" \
    || buc_die "Local build failed for ${RBRV_SIGIL}"

  # Create fake vouch tag (same image, aliased — satisfies rbob_charge vouch gate)
  buc_step "Creating vouch tag"
  docker tag "${z_image_ref}" "${z_vouch_ref}" \
    || buc_die "Failed to create vouch tag"

  # Persist facts — mirror conjure/bind/graft so downstream consumers (theurge)
  # can build full refs uniformly regardless of mode.
  buf_write_fact_single "${RBF_FACT_HALLMARK}" "${z_hallmark}"

1	# Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc.
2	# SPDX-License-Identifier: Apache-2.0
3	#
4	# Recipe Bottle base compose file — static infrastructure
5	#
6	# Invoked via:
7	#   docker compose --env-file rbmm_moorings/rbrr.env --env-file rbmm_moorings/rbrd.env \
8	#     --env-file rbmm_moorings/{moniker}/rbrn.env \
9	#     -f Tools/rbk/rbob_compose.yml [-f rbmm_moorings/{moniker}/rbnnh_compose.yml] -p {moniker} up -d
10	#
11	# Env-file variables serve dual purpose:
12	#   1. YAML interpolation (${VAR} in this file — image refs, subnet, container names)
13	#   2. Container environment forwarding (bare names in environment: sections)
14	#
15	# Network topology:
16	#   sentry: transit (uplink to internet via bridge) + enclave (down to bottle)
17	#     Interface naming and default-route assignment are NOT controlled by compose:
18	#       - eth0/eth1 ordering is Docker-engine-defined (sentry IP-discovers via RBRN_ENCLAVE_SENTRY_IP)
